posts.title: Migrant Mother, 1936
posts.text:
Florence Owens Thompson, 32 years old, sitting in a lean-to tent at a pea-pickers' camp in Nipomo, California. March 1936. Three of her seven children are in the frame.

Dorothea Lange almost drove past the camp. She was heading home after a month of fieldwork for the FSA. She turned around after going ten miles. Spent ten minutes at the tent. Shot six frames.

Thompson told Lange the family had been living on frozen vegetables from the surrounding fields and birds the children killed. The pea crop had frozen. There was no work.

Lange's editor at the San Francisco News published the photo two days later. The federal government sent 20,000 pounds of food to the camp.

Thompson wasn't identified until 1978. She said she never received a cent from the photo.

Dorothea Lange, Migrant Mother, 1936. Library of Congress, FSA/OWI Collection.

posts.title: Dust Storm, Cimarron County, Oklahoma, 1936
posts.text:
Arthur Rothstein, April 1936. Cimarron County, Oklahoma. A farmer and his two sons walking toward their shed while a wall of dust rolls in behind them.

Cimarron County was ground zero. The driest, most wind-eroded county in the Oklahoma Panhandle. By 1936, the topsoil was gone. What's in this photo isn't dirt — it's the pulverized remains of farmland that blew away because the grass that held it was plowed under for wheat.

Rothstein was 21 years old and three months into his FSA assignment. This was his fifth roll. He shot four frames of this scene. The first three are throwaway. The fourth one — this one — is the single most reproduced photograph of the Dust Bowl.

The family's name was Coble. Arthur Coble and his sons Darrel and Milton. They stayed.

Arthur Rothstein, Dust Storm, Cimarron County, 1936. Library of Congress, FSA/OWI Collection.

posts.title: Sharecropper's Family, Hale County, Alabama, 1936
posts.text:
Floyd and Lucille Burroughs on their porch. Hale County, Alabama, summer 1936. Walker Evans shot this during an eight-week stay with three sharecropper families for Fortune magazine.

Fortune killed the story. Too depressing. James Agee used the material for "Let Us Now Praise Famous Men" instead. Published in 1941, it sold 600 copies. By 1960 it was considered one of the most important American books of the century.

Evans used an 8x10 view camera. No flash. Natural light only. He posed his subjects the way a portrait painter would — facing the camera, dead center, no pretense that this was candid.

The Burroughs family earned about $100 a year from cotton. They owed most of it to the landowner before the crop was even picked.

Walker Evans, 1936. Library of Congress, FSA/OWI Collection.

posts.title: American Gothic, Washington D.C., 1942
posts.text:
Ella Watson standing with a broom and mop in front of an American flag. Washington, D.C., August 1942. Gordon Parks's first assignment for the FSA.

Parks was 30, the only Black photographer on Roy Stryker's team. Stryker told him to go photograph Washington. Parks walked into a government building, tried to eat at the cafeteria, was turned away. Tried to see a movie at a theater. Turned away. Tried to buy a coat at a department store. Turned away.

He went back to the office furious. Stryker gave him a camera and told him to photograph the cleaning woman. Parks posed Watson the way Grant Wood posed his Iowa farmer — straight on, tools of her trade, the flag behind her.

Watson earned $1,080 a year cleaning the building where Parks wasn't allowed to eat.

Gordon Parks, American Gothic, Washington D.C., 1942. Library of Congress, FSA/OWI Collection.

posts.title: Migrant Family on Highway, Oklahoma to California, 1937
posts.text:
A family on U.S. Route 66, somewhere between Oklahoma and California, 1937. Dorothea Lange. They're walking. The car broke down or they never had one.

Between 1930 and 1940, about 2.5 million people left the Great Plains states. Most went to California. They called them Okies regardless of where they actually came from — Oklahoma, Texas, Arkansas, Missouri, Kansas.

California didn't want them. In February 1936, the Los Angeles police chief sent 136 officers to the state border to turn back anyone who looked poor. They called it the "bum blockade." It lasted two months until the ACLU sued.

The FSA set up migrant camps with running water and sanitation. The camps were controversial. Locals said they attracted more migrants. The migrants said it was the first running water their children had ever used.

Dorothea Lange, 1937. Library of Congress, FSA/OWI Collection.
